reasoning: The Lean statement deviates from the informal one in two opposing ways. First, it restricts A to SQUARE matrices (`Matrix n n R` with the same index type for rows and columns), whereas the informal "Let A be a matrix" imposes no squareness — a general homogeneous system Ax=0 is m×n. This restriction makes Lean cover fewer cases than the informal. Second, the informal explicitly says "k ∈ ℝ" and implicitly treats entries as real numbers, but the Lean generalizes R to an arbitrary field, covering more cases than the informal. Since one deviation narrows the scope (matrix shape) and the other broadens it (field), neither statement implies the other: the informal (about any-shape matrices over ℝ) does not imply the Lean (about square matrices over any field), and the Lean does not imply the informal. Hence they are incomparable.
